title: Students Asked to Return Money for Weekend Classes
summary: Over 20,000 students who take weekend classes have been told they got money they shouldn't have. This money is called maintenance loans and grants, which help pay for things like food and housing while studying. The Student Loans Company sent letters saying the students' courses were not allowed to get this money because they only meet on weekends. Now, the students must pay back the money. Some students also got extra help for childcare, which they might not have to repay. The universities say it's not their fault, but the government says there was a mistake or someone broke the rules. Students are upset and can ask for help if paying back is hard.

background_read: This article is about a problem with student funding in the UK. Students who study on weekends received money called maintenance loans and grants to help with costs like housing and food. However, it was later found that their courses were not supposed to get this money because they only meet on weekends. Now, over 20,000 students are being asked to pay back the money, which can be very stressful. The universities and the government disagree on who is at fault, and students are seeking help if they cannot afford to repay.
Article_Structure: WHO: Over 20,000 students taking weekend courses
WHAT: They were told to pay back maintenance loans and grants given by mistake
WHERE: At 15 universities and colleges in the UK, like London Met and Oxford Brookes
WHY: Because their courses were not eligible for the funding due to being weekend-only classes

perspectives.description: Students are devastated and facing financial hardship because they have to repay money they thought was theirs to use for living costs.
Universities blame the government for an abrupt decision and are considering legal action, saying they provided correct information.
The government says the mistake was due to incompetence or abuse of the system by universities, and students were let down.
